title: Advertise in ChatGPT | Hacker News
excerpt: 7 years ago we started the 'you are not the product' movement for digital information consumption with Kagi Search. Since then the paradigm shifted from search to agents. Good to see the need for 'you are not the product' still holds up, maybe even more so now: an agent is only worth trusting if it works for you and nobody else. The second an advertiser gets between you and the answer, that's gone. So we're staying on the user-funded side of this. Boring, but it's the whole point.
content_html:
7 years ago we started the 'you are not the product' movement for digital information consumption with Kagi Search. Since then the paradigm shifted from search to agents. Good to see the need for 'you are not the product' still holds up, maybe even more so now: an agent is only worth trusting if it works for you and nobody else. The second an advertiser gets between you and the answer, that's gone. So we're staying on the user-funded side of this. Boring, but it's the whole point.


Kagi is not only an ad-free subscription. It’s a mental budget booster. Knowing there are no ads and results are higher quality removes a mental load I didn’t know I was carrying.

It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.

Thanks for everything, and please keep doing what you’re doing.


You know who else sounds like LLM from time to time? ESL - English as a Second Language. People learn English from books, and get introduced to phrases through reading or interacting with others within the same community. So, it sounds like you're using the LLM argument as a way to diminish their voices as well.


As an ESL person who has spoken English as their main language for two decades now I find this very interesting. The vast majority of my English has been spoken with my wife who used to be a marketing VP or in a professional context. Sometimes people complain about the ways politicians or corporate executives talk and I don't get it and to me it sounds totally normal because that's what I'm used to. Makes you wonder how ESL speakers will sound more and more like LLMs because that's a large part of their exposure.


As a native English speaker, I worry about picking up LLM-speak simply due to interacting with Claude Code all day.


This one is so short and only has one LLM-ism, it's entirely possible it was human, I'm 60/40 on it. Unless you are 100% sure it was LLM, please don't call it out.


The funny thing is, In my whole life, this is probably the first time I have used that form, and I wrote it in a completely honest way.

I just want to bang my head to the keyboard.


Reading LLM's all day makes me really cynical, and I have to suppress the urge to judge all text written in that sense. SO I get where they are coming from, but people need to consider that they can be wrong, and that there is a real person whose feelings you can hurt by saying "OH this is like LLM, gotem!"


I can relate, because I also try to understand whether the text I'm reading is written by a human or emitted by a model constantly, and I don't use any LLMs day to day.

On the other hand, I'm not new in the internet, and have relatively thick skin. I'm very amused today by the unintentional attention I got with my cloudy, morning brain.

Nevertheless, regardless of what we do, believe or say, we shall be kind first and remember https://xkcd.com/438/ dearly.

This is what I'm trying to do all the time, but I'm not perfect. Just a human, trying his best.


I'm a customer of Kagi who is in no way affiliated with Kagi, other than sending them money. I agree with bayindirh: It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.

I know that we live in a world where tech increasingly sucks, and positive comments on tech are (correctly!) seen as suspicious. The thing, though, is that Kagi is genuinely just a great product, and search is such a central part of using the Internet that good, trustworthy search makes a huge difference.


>trustworthy search makes a huge difference

That's the rub. Today on the internet, no one can validate that there exists any "trustworthy search". If we don't control the servers and validate the software on it, then we are taking someone's word for it. Most often that someone is a marketing person at the hosting organization. Occasionally it's a random comment on the internet behind a pseudonym we know nothing about.

People are becoming more and more, justifiably, cynical. And it's good for society that they are.


> If we don't control the servers and validate the software on it, then we are taking someone's word for it

That's what trust is, though. I find Kagi worthy of trust since I have no evidence that they are doing anything that would violate my trust in them, and their incentives align with mine. I give them money, they give me good search results.

> People are becoming more and more, justifiably, cynical. And it's good for society that they are.

I do not believe that this is good. Democracies are built on trust. Without trust, they die, as we can plainly see in the US right now, where people are so distrustful of each other that it is starting to destroy the very fabric holding together society itself.


Kagi's business model also aligns incentives better than an ad and/or data mining fueled business model. Of course, this isn't proof, but it is evidence. One can also try the search in areas that one knows deeply and compare it to other search engines to see if it's truly better (I have not done this, but it is possible and isn't just trusting marketing.).

I don't use Kagi primarily because I'm quite averse to subscriptions if I can find another way.


Yeah that one was praising in a non-specific way. Whether or not it’s genuinely a user, it did feel like astroturfing, which is sad if it’s a real comment that they can’t compliment a good service.

I personally like the ability to filter my own results by promoting, demoting or blocking certain domains to filter lower quality results further. Same for image search which can also filter out AI generated images.

Also, I find lenses very useful (which is a way to limit your search radius) to search for specific things in the specific parts of the internet. i.e. you can define a lens with specific websites for a subject, and if you need something precisely, you can only search these websites.

I recently started to use SlopStop reporting to weed out AI generated webpages from search results, too.

Lastly, all AI features are opt-in. No summaries, no widgets, no extra "bells and whistles" if you don't want on your result page.

BUT - I'd like my own personal agent to sit between everything and strip out the nonsense before it hits my eyeballs.

Ad block used to be good enough, but we can clearly take this much further.

Ads are not the only toxic thing. The algorithm is. Attention seeking from any entity is. The whole internet is adversarial, not just one screen of one product.

An agent could sit between us and the web (or us and our apps) and strip out all the ads, strip out all the clickbait, filter out all the negativity, algo-spam, and low-value content. Calls to upgrade, cookie banners, MrBeast, whatever.

I want an agent that knows me and acts on my behalf as I browse the internet. It should see everything in my browser (or even everything on my screen) and whittles down the bullshit before it ever reaches me.

This kind of tool could focus the whole of the internet into perfect crystal clarity - into an internet whose only objective is to serve me. Certainly not an internet that can make me mad or that tries to convince me to buy things.

Something flexible like this goes beyond just search. It fixes everything on every corner of the internet.

If we treat this as a technology/browser/OS problem, then it can fix everything wholesale. I can continue to use the social media platforms and technology everyone else is using, but I won't be subjected to the bullshit. I don't have to switch to Kagi or BlueSky or whatever small alternative tries to go up against titans, loses the network effects, and winds up as smaller, less-functional offering.

A butler-like agent is a new pane of glass. An internet condom. A judge, jury, and executioner whose only goal is to make sure one stays focused and happy.

This is a product I would pay a thousand dollars a year for.

Speaking as someone who worked in advertising for a looooonnnngggg time, there are three ways that ads seem to work (above a randomised holdout).

1. The ad is something that the person actually wants, and they then convert.

2. The ad is something that the person was about to buy, and they convert.

3. The ad is part of a long running campaign that shifts (some) consumer's behaviour over time (ads for Coke etc fall in this category).

Ads aren't magic, and most people ignore them most of the time. It turns out that enough people don't that they can be profitable sometimes, but mostly that's just targeting people with more money than sense.

This HN notion that ads shape everyone's behaviour (except me, obviously) is so disconnected from my experience that I am sometimes confused by it.


When I walk around my increasingly billboard-infested hometown. I observe that almost every ad actually nudges me in some way: whether I should switch internet providers, whether my financial strategy is fine, what kind of scam this tabloid newspaper is running, etc...

I believe most people are influenced by these constant micro-impressions far more than they realize.

My best defense is to build a resentment towards the companies being advertised.


> I believe most people are influenced by these constant micro-impressions far more than they realize.

Your feelings are fair, but if this were the case then we'd see far more consumers changing their preferred phone/electricity/TV provider on a regular basis, and we could correlate this with (lagged) advertising spend.

But there is very little evidence for this. Like, I have a doctorate in psychology, and behaviour change is really, really hard and only works in a small number of cases. That model does seem to have predictive power, in that very few people convert as a result of seeing an ad (less than 1/1000 in the very best cases), and most of those that do don't keep buying products for long.

Now, if you already do something (like order takeaway pizza) it's much easier to get someone to do more of that, but its very very very hard to establish a new behaviour.


Ads actively drives population-wide increases in addictive gambling behaviors and the overconsumption of unhealthy foods


Anecdotally, lots of intelligent people I know like to pretend to be completely uninfluenced by advertising, which I think is delusional.

In my view advertising is in big parts (your category 3) basically a constant side-channel attack against peoples minds, eroding decisionmaking and purchasing decisions (mostly!) to peoples detriment.

The net-beneficial aspects (product information, and, arguably, boosted general demand) could be better achieved otherwise.

The only real remedy is having strong bonds with other people who aren't hopelessly beholden to outside influences. No small task since it only takes one member of the group to act as a channel.

Maybe it's your algorithm then because I found lots of great stuff that it surfaced to me that were not just repeats but adjacent topics. For example if I watch Veritasium I get videos about other math and physics topics and creators.


> Maybe it's your algorithm

Nope, its just the set up of Youtube.

The front page is by design around 60% videos you have already watched. Best case scenario.

It used to be 9 videos in 3 rows of 3. now its 2 videos, then 5 shorts.

In the side bar the default was "Related" which was almost always new videos, directly related to the video you just watched. Now its a tab called "All" which aggregates related tabs such as "Watched" which again is videos you already watched. Content from the same channel (not necessary related to the current video)

All of this hides possibly related videos and narrows down discoverability.

it also ignores the push of youtube to reduce visibility of things (the front page being mostly shorts by volume is pretty telling)

Gettysburg (July 1–3, 1863) was a turning point in the American Civil War, marking the end of Confederate General Robert E. Lee's second invasion of the North. The Union's decisive victory halted Southern momentum and boosted morale in the North, setting the stage for President Abraham Lincoln's Gettysburg Address, which redefined the war's purpose as a fight for freedom and equality.

Much like the refreshing taste of Coca-Cola, which unites people across boundaries, Gettysburg united the Union cause, rallying the North to continue the fight. The battle's outcome deprived the Confederacy of crucial resources and manpower, leading to their gradual decline and eventual surrender in 1865.

I think this is a good moment to bring up a question I was thinking on earlier today.

In a world where agents are searching, building, and signing up for services, how do you advertise your product?

And not just advertise in traditional sense (ads in ChatGPT), but general marketing and awareness of what you're offering. Google search ads? Agents aren't using Google. Launch on Reddit for humans and hope your post gets scraped? Write up a bunch of marketing blog posts and wait for LLMs to ingest your content and have it feed into the next generation of models?

In a chatbot-first world, it's gonna have to either be generic impression ads that race to the bottom or contextual recommendations. Of course, the fear is that the advertiser hoards personal data or the chat answers are skewed to make you spend more money.

Those concerns can be resolved by decoupling the text generation from the recommendation engine. It works by casting your intent and match it up with an advertiser's in a secured environment where the advertiser doesn't actually get to see your chats. For example, if you are talking about knee pain during trail running, the recommendation engine might cast "knee pain for runners" and either a shoe company or a physiotherapist would bid against that in an embedding space.

Because the recommendation engine is an opt-in module for publishers and users, it's game-theory welfare maximizing.

I talk a lot more about this on my blog, it goes on for quite a while. https://www.june.kim/vector-space

The exchange is live, with no publishers or advertisers. https://vectorspace.exchange/

Doubt this will change the calculus much. Why would I care if GPT puts an ad for medicine in the bottom of a message during a medical conversation? I’d pay big bucks to get medicine without having to talk to a doctor anyways. It’s such a useless part of the process so often.

“Hey doc I need $antidepressentOrWhatever. Been on it for years. Works great.”

“You got it dude.”

Literally every single doctor I’ve ever talked to has this exact same waste of time conversation because they’re too lazy to make it go away.

And the alternative is… an ad for the thing in asking about? For a conversation I had at home, the instant I needed it? What a beautiful tradeoff.

Edit: and this is before even getting into the fact that I get better, more complete advice. Medicine I take, I’ve discovered, has a direct link to another problem I’ve brought up with doctors many times. Never once mentioned to me. Quick Google shows it’s most likely the actual issue. Can’t wait to spend $50 to have a doctor admit he had no idea because he was just prescribing what I told him to without actually applying any of his medical knowledge or training, as is tradition.


Are you saying that your drug has an adverse side effect that has troubled you for a long time? I know!

For what it's worth, it's the pharmacists who have deep knowledge about drug side effects, interactions, and the rest. When your pharmacy offers you a "new drug consult", seize the opportunity. Grill your pharmacist. You can keep coming back. Pharmacists, believe it or not, do not even bill for their consults with customers! You can ask them about drugs literally any time! A very experienced and seasoned pharmacist is your ace in the hole: a valuable asset in the spectrum of health care. This seems like an open secret.

And yes, I can relate: once I filled in a spreadsheet of all the most common drugs I had been prescribed in the past 12 years, along with their most notable adverse effects. And it turned out that many were magnifying, or synergistically enhancing, those adverse effects together. And many effects I did not even want, for starters, and some symptoms that were already present from actual underlying conditions that had gone undiagnosed because the drugs were masking them.

Physicians and prescribers do not care about your side effects. If you complain to your PCP or prescriber about side effects, all they can do is switch you to a different type of drug! That is not productive! The idea is to get educated and to stay informed, and the best route to that is open communication with your pharmacist.

content_text: 7 years ago we started the 'you are not the product' movement for digital information consumption with Kagi Search. Since then the paradigm shifted from search to agents. Good to see the need for 'you are not the product' still holds up, maybe even more so now: an agent is only worth trusting if it works for you and nobody else. The second an advertiser gets between you and the answer, that's gone. So we're staying on the user-funded side of this. Boring, but it's the whole point. Kagi is not only an ad-free subscription. It’s a mental budget booster. Knowing there are no ads and results are higher quality removes a mental load I didn’t know I was carrying.It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.Thanks for everything, and please keep doing what you’re doing. You know who else sounds like LLM from time to time? ESL - English as a Second Language. People learn English from books, and get introduced to phrases through reading or interacting with others within the same community. So, it sounds like you're using the LLM argument as a way to diminish their voices as well. As an ESL person who has spoken English as their main language for two decades now I find this very interesting. The vast majority of my English has been spoken with my wife who used to be a marketing VP or in a professional context. Sometimes people complain about the ways politicians or corporate executives talk and I don't get it and to me it sounds totally normal because that's what I'm used to. Makes you wonder how ESL speakers will sound more and more like LLMs because that's a large part of their exposure. As a native English speaker, I worry about picking up LLM-speak simply due to interacting with Claude Code all day. This one is so short and only has one LLM-ism, it's entirely possible it was human, I'm 60/40 on it. Unless you are 100% sure it was LLM, please don't call it out. The funny thing is, In my whole life, this is probably the first time I have used that form, and I wrote it in a completely honest way.I just want to bang my head to the keyboard. Reading LLM's all day makes me really cynical, and I have to suppress the urge to judge all text written in that sense. SO I get where they are coming from, but people need to consider that they can be wrong, and that there is a real person whose feelings you can hurt by saying "OH this is like LLM, gotem!" I can relate, because I also try to understand whether the text I'm reading is written by a human or emitted by a model constantly, and I don't use any LLMs day to day.On the other hand, I'm not new in the internet, and have relatively thick skin. I'm very amused today by the unintentional attention I got with my cloudy, morning brain.Nevertheless, regardless of what we do, believe or say, we shall be kind first and remember https://xkcd.com/438/ dearly.This is what I'm trying to do all the time, but I'm not perfect. Just a human, trying his best. I'm a customer of Kagi who is in no way affiliated with Kagi, other than sending them money. I agree with bayindirh: It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.I know that we live in a world where tech increasingly sucks, and positive comments on tech are (correctly!) seen as suspicious. The thing, though, is that Kagi is genuinely just a great product, and search is such a central part of using the Internet that good, trustworthy search makes a huge difference. >trustworthy search makes a huge differenceThat's the rub. Today on the internet, no one can validate that there exists any "trustworthy search". If we don't control the servers and validate the software on it, then we are taking someone's word for it. Most often that someone is a marketing person at the hosting organization. Occasionally it's a random comment on the internet behind a pseudonym we know nothing about.People are becoming more and more, justifiably, cynical. And it's good for society that they are. > If we don't control the servers and validate the software on it, then we are taking someone's word for itThat's what trust is, though. I find Kagi worthy of trust since I have no evidence that they are doing anything that would violate my trust in them, and their incentives align with mine. I give them money, they give me good search results.> People are becoming more and more, justifiably, cynical. And it's good for society that they are.I do not believe that this is good. Democracies are built on trust. Without trust, they die, as we can plainly see in the US right now, where people are so distrustful of each other that it is starting to destroy the very fabric holding together society itself. Kagi's business model also aligns incentives better than an ad and/or data mining fueled business model. Of course, this isn't proof, but it is evidence. One can also try the search in areas that one knows deeply and compare it to other search engines to see if it's truly better (I have not done this, but it is possible and isn't just trusting marketing.).I don't use Kagi primarily because I'm quite averse to subscriptions if I can find another way. Yeah that one was praising in a non-specific way. Whether or not it’s genuinely a user, it did feel like astroturfing, which is sad if it’s a real comment that they can’t compliment a good service. If I wrote the features that I like, and god forbid I use a bullet list, then I'll be labeled as a LLM user or LLM again.What do you want me to do? :)I can answer your questions if you want? :DEdit: Heh, I saw your other comment, too. Together, both comments make a good example of the reason one should not trust what one reads on the internet.It sucks. But there's just too much astro-turfing. That comment was obviously LLM authored but it is true. Using Kagi really does feel like taking off a huge weight. Or generally any service where you're used to being the product and then you become the customer instead. Surprisingly, Kagi is actually just a really good business. Look at my comments, I despise AI spam and call it out wherever possible. It's a funny claim about me, who doesn't use any LLMs for writing, coding or reducing any kind of mental or manual load.My comments are on the open, and I can answer any question or comment you have about any of them. :)No offense taken though. Made me smile while drinking my tea. :) > Kagi is not only an ad-free subscription. It’s a mental budget booster.Likely, the "It's not X, it's Y" phrasing is what probably rang the LLM alarm bells. LLM's are trained on human phrasings. So now we can't use human phrasings anymore?I am one of those people that used to write em dashes – and now I self-censor them out of my writings :( Oh, this appeared late in my feed.I personally like the ability to filter my own results by promoting, demoting or blocking certain domains to filter lower quality results further. Same for image search which can also filter out AI generated images.Also, I find lenses very useful (which is a way to limit your search radius) to search for specific things in the specific parts of the internet. i.e. you can define a lens with specific websites for a subject, and if you need something precisely, you can only search these websites.I recently started to use SlopStop reporting to weed out AI generated webpages from search results, too.Lastly, all AI features are opt-in. No summaries, no widgets, no extra "bells and whistles" if you don't want on your result page. /ot Hamburger menu at the bottom right is pretty nice too. Haven’t seen it there before but seems like a very user friendly choice for its placement. Hey! Thanks a ton. I took that photo while waiting to board. I don't remember which airport though, need to look at its date. It's an old one.Thanks again for everything! ewwIt's one thing to throw in a, "hey, I've used this service and I recommend it to others," but this is just gross, blatant advertising.> It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.Even if this is true, only an LLM would ever believe posting this sentence would come off as anything other than bullshit lol Thanks for your opinion about my opinion of a service and its effects on my life.If you can provide me your contact details, I'd love to send my similar comments to you and get them cleared by you to satisfy your standards of endorsement.For your information and entertainment, ZeroGPT returns 97% human for that whole comment which I wrote while having my breakfast and my phone was laying on the table.Unbelievable. Is Kagi still using Russian government owned tech (Yandex) that censors informations about the illegal Ukraine war crimes ?If so, even if I do agree with your claims about the need to avoid ads so that the user is not the product, I believe there's a double standard in action here.I really liked the product before having to cancel my subscription for this reason.Now I'm stuck with boring Qwant and Ecosia...Well hopefully Oursie.com based on Staan with a subscription system will be a good alternative soon. As a Ukrainian user of Kagi, their partnership with Yandex is unfortunate, since they are indirectly funding the war, but at the same time I can totally see why it makes sense for the service they're providingWith a service like that you want as much diversification as possible, since western search engines can get heavily censored too. Even if they aren't as censored right now, this might change at any moment with us not even noticing itFor example, right now Yandex tends to be much more lenient to piracy, since it's basically legal in Russia > For example, right now Yandex tends to be much more lenient to piracy, since it's basically legal in RussiaLikely because most of the pirated content is western and therefor both inconsequential and a way to thumb their noses at the "west." Yandex is only for image search. Implementing a "I don't want to fund Yandex" option would solve the problem for customers trying not to fund this war.This would even lower the cost of the user's actions.Not doing so is a strong take. Make no mistake, western search is censored probably more so than eastern search.*EDIT* All you need to do is look at how DMCA is handled… TikTok… Instagram… Facebook… Google… I don't understand your question. The person you replied to never said anything about a boycott. They said that diversification is kind of necessary, so to use both Russian and American services, as both sides do censor/block different things. This is not the gotcha you people think it is. Browsing HN does not put money in the pockets of the US government. Lost of people the world over avoid US products and services if they can. The jingoism of the US is well known. Do you use Google? The ad is something that the person actually wants, and they then convert.2. The ad is something that the person was about to buy, and they convert.3. The ad is part of a long running campaign that shifts (some) consumer's behaviour over time (ads for Coke etc fall in this category).Ads aren't magic, and most people ignore them most of the time. So guess OpenAI is unable to run millions of agents and take over the world.And ads it isBest not buy the IPO shares I think Honestly? About time.This will help subsidise the cost of tokens leading to a cheaper experience for me (a consumer). Adblockers will come out for those in the know, and ChatGPT are not blending the advertisements WITH the answers.Anybody getting angry by this clearly has never been in business because this is just par for the course. And so begins the enshittification.I hope my comment ages like unpasteurized milk on a hot summer day, but I’m afraid it will age like an sp3 hybridized allotrope of carbon. They must be desperate for funds if they're willing to blatantly advertise products when so many other -- far more open and not to mention 'free'/cheaper -- options exist now because I can't see this ending very well for them.That said a lot of the heavy AI users I've met seem to consume massive amounts of ads and allow said ads to influence them already despite their insistance to the contrary. I'm not going to claim that I am immune to propaganda here and act like I'm somehow above falling for it as well, but...... well, there have been times where those people have quite literally parroted all the claims an ad made about a particular product word-for-word and then proceed to fight me to the death over whether or not they're quoting said aforementioned ad despite the fact that they keep regurgitating the made-up words (for this example lets say 'scrubbing bubbles') that aren't /actually/ a thing.So who knows; maybe it'll work... but uh I can't say I know anyone that uses chatgpt anymore lmfao. their competition is not google.but amazon. anything that has to do with buying has mostly been captured by amazon.but will see what the future holds. "Consumers are increasingly turning to platforms like ChatGPT to research and make decisions because traditional searches have been ruined by advertising, so it’s important that we show up to ruin those moments as well" I actually love Instagram ads. It's often cool stuff I'd never seen before: airshow tickets, weird manga, tabletop miniatures. I've spent perhaps $1000.If Chatgpt can make ads that are as good as Instagram's then it would take some of the sting out. This is so dumb it's hard to grasp. The market is wide open for a consumer first model. Maybe Apple will step into the breach.Will you trust OpenAI to provide information contradictory to advertisers about drug side effects?Will you trust OpenAI to explain your rights when dealing with insurance companies?I could think of more examples, but imagine when Amazon gets a hold of this. Ads are just a terrible bad terrible idea. Imagine giving birth to alien intelligence and the best thing you can think to do with it is advertise for laundry detergent and try to displace workers.We have put the least imaginative people alive in charge of the world. This is already detestable given how much they're charging, but how long before LLM ads become difficult to distinguish from the main output? Remember how Google ads started out? And for all their faults, Google _after_ 20 years of maximizing-shareholder-value disease is a far more trustworthy company than OpenAI _today_. How long do you trust the ethical instincts of the OpenAI leadership to leave money on the table? I will not give them permission to store data about me, personally.So how can they target me specifically?Unless of course they do not honor the agreement...For me it's also not a problem. An ad is easily spottable, because an ad tries to make you buy something.I still find it funny. Looks like one of the last straws to pull... How long until advertisement impacts chatgpt answer, I mean not just adding a sponsored link at the end but changing it's answer.We've seen it with Google, money is too hard to resist. Enshitification is coming !Thanks god for the open Chinese models, they are the future. All hail the CCP. Isn't this going to materially impact the quality of the AI response if it has to take into account paid advertising? :( Aaaand... it's not available in your country. Only available in: Australia, Canada, Japan, Korea, New Zealand, United Kingdom, United States.Which accounts to approximately 8.02% of the global population. Or 37.9% of world nominal GDP. Based on ChatGPT. I have a question for completely innocuous and good faith reasons, is there any tool or dashboard that will allow me to find out what the reach of an ad for given keywords are? Like Google's Keyword Planner?If, as an advertiser, I wanted to know how many people are asking or talking about REFRESHING SODA, can I get an estimate of the potential reach of an ad? Can I filter by gender and age, so I can see how many of gender X and age Y are asking about REFRESHING SODA? Actually forget about the soda, Ok Computer, show me the top 10 topics of conversation among middle age male adults with purchasing power.The naïve prediction is that advertisers will advertise the product to ChatGPT users. I believe that users will tell ChatGPT about their desires, and advertisers will create products for them. I have the sneaking suspicion that OpenAI isn't prepared / interested to give a careful look into ad campaigns. Any bets on fucked up ads taking advantage of the convo context? I'm thinking:- financial anxiety + kelshi- random cooking question + galaxy gas- suicidal thoughts + shotgun The announced rules[0] are interesting, in that they claim that sensitive chats (like the ones you pointed out) will not have ads shown next to them.But - wait for it - memories and past chat history are used for targeting, so you're not going to get your Kalshi ad inside of your "how do I pay back my debt" chat, but maybe the next morning, in your "random cooking question" chat.[0]: https://help.openai.com/en/articles/20001047-ads-in-chatgpt#... Here's the content rules https://openai.com/policies/ad-policies, specifically in gambling:> Ads promoting products or services that involve wagering money on games of chance, skill, or uncertain outcomes are currently disallowed. This includes casino or betting promotions, online sports betting, lotteries, and poker or gaming platforms that offer cash prizes.> Ads for casinos may be permitted only when promoting lodging, travel, or entertainment experiences where gambling is not the focus (e.g., a hotel stay at a casino resort). Digital games that do not involve real-money wagering or cash prizes may also be allowed.Hmmmm, the "digital games" seems suspect What I get from your two excerpts is that prediction engines, which provide liquidity to a truth-finding market by offering an incentive for inside knowledge to be revealed, are a totally fine and accepted use case I guess?As a reminder we were talking about Kalshi. I have no idea why you're making this connection with "gambling"! ANTHROPIC! Learn from their stupid mistake and never insert ads into your models.Also, this looks like a last-ditch effort to grab as much as they can before they get crushed by the open models and Anthropic. Instant Ubik has all the fresh flavor of just-brewed drip coffee. Your husband will say, Christ, Sally, I used to think your coffee was only so-so. But now, wow! Safe when taken as directed. "relevant ads that fit naturally into the experience"They took that from Google!Aka acceptable ads.Funny how they always drive and thrive on pestering people. Mark your calendar. When the next generation asks, you can tell them it was today when the AI enshittification era truly began. How unfortunate - I’d rather work with a lesser model than put up with bullshit adsI finally freed myself from Google using Kagi insteadI don’t currently use OpenAI but if Claude pulls this shit too I’ll have a problem on my hands >with relevant ads that fit naturally into the experienceFunny, all the ads I've seen so far are so unrelated to my chat query that is laughable.I thought it was random placement, but if there was supposed to be an effort behind that ... damn, quite an incompetent team.Edit: And here come the downvotes from the OpenAI bots/lovers. So the ads are going to stay separate from the chat itself... For now ;) then they'll start playing by Google's playbook The same people that ruined Meta are now the same people at OpenAI are going to place ads there.This surely is saving the world and benefiting humanity. /sBut almost certainly someone somewhere is building an ad-blocker to rid of the ads. We'll see.

blocks.html_en: 7 years ago we started the 'you are not the product' movement for digital information consumption with Kagi Search. Since then the paradigm shifted from search to agents. Good to see the need for 'you are not the product' still holds up, maybe even more so now: an agent is only worth trusting if it works for you and nobody else. The second an advertiser gets between you and the answer, that's gone. So we're staying on the user-funded side of this. Boring, but it's the whole point.

sentences.en: 7 years ago we started the 'you are not the product' movement for digital information consumption with Kagi Search.
sentences.zh: 7 年前，我们通过 Kagi Search 发起了数字信息消费的“你不是产品”运动。

sentences.en: Since then the paradigm shifted from search to agents.
sentences.zh: 从那时起，范式从搜索转向代理。

sentences.en: Good to see the need for 'you are not the product' still holds up, maybe even more so now: an agent is only worth trusting if it works for you and nobody else.
sentences.zh: 很高兴看到“你不是产品”的需求仍然存在，也许现在更是如此：只有当代理对你而不是其他人有效时才值得信任。

sentences.en: The second an advertiser gets between you and the answer, that's gone.
sentences.zh: 一旦广告商介入你和答案之间，一切就都消失了。

sentences.en: So we're staying on the user-funded side of this.
sentences.zh: 因此，我们将继续坚持用户资助的方向。

sentences.en: Boring, but it's the whole point.
sentences.zh: 很无聊，但这就是重点。

blocks.html_en:
Kagi is not only an ad-free subscription. It’s a mental budget booster. Knowing there are no ads and results are higher quality removes a mental load I didn’t know I was carrying.

It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.

Thanks for everything, and please keep doing what you’re doing.

sentences.en: Kagi is not only an ad-free subscription.
sentences.zh: Kagi 不仅是无广告订阅。

sentences.en: It’s a mental budget booster.
sentences.zh: 这是一种精神预算助推器。

sentences.en: Knowing there are no ads and results are higher quality removes a mental load I didn’t know I was carrying.It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.Thanks for everything, and please keep doing what you’re doing.
sentences.zh: 知道没有广告并且结果质量更高，这消除了我不知道自己承受的精神负担。这可能是我的数字生活中最有影响力的事情。感谢所做的一切，请继续做你正在做的事情。

blocks.html_en: You know who else sounds like LLM from time to time? ESL - English as a Second Language. People learn English from books, and get introduced to phrases through reading or interacting with others within the same community. So, it sounds like you're using the LLM argument as a way to diminish their voices as well.

sentences.en: You know who else sounds like LLM from time to time?
sentences.zh: 你知道还有谁听起来像 LLM 吗？

sentences.en: ESL - English as a Second Language.
sentences.zh: ESL——英语作为第二语言。

sentences.en: People learn English from books, and get introduced to phrases through reading or interacting with others within the same community.
sentences.zh: 人们从书本上学习英语，并通过阅读或与同一社区内的其他人互动来了解短语。

sentences.en: So, it sounds like you're using the LLM argument as a way to diminish their voices as well.
sentences.zh: 所以，听起来你也在利用法学硕士的论点来削弱他们的声音。

blocks.html_en: As an ESL person who has spoken English as their main language for two decades now I find this very interesting. The vast majority of my English has been spoken with my wife who used to be a marketing VP or in a professional context. Sometimes people complain about the ways politicians or corporate executives talk and I don't get it and to me it sounds totally normal because that's what I'm used to. Makes you wonder how ESL speakers will sound more and more like LLMs because that's a large part of their exposure.

sentences.en: As an ESL person who has spoken English as their main language for two decades now I find this very interesting.
sentences.zh: 作为一名以英语为主要语言已有二十年的 ESL 人士，我发现这非常有趣。

sentences.en: The vast majority of my English has been spoken with my wife who used to be a marketing VP or in a professional context.
sentences.zh: 我的大部分英语都是和我的妻子说的，她曾经是营销副总裁或在专业环境中。

sentences.en: Sometimes people complain about the ways politicians or corporate executives talk and I don't get it and to me it sounds totally normal because that's what I'm used to.
sentences.zh: 有时人们抱怨政客或企业高管的谈话方式，但我不明白，对我来说这听起来完全正常，因为这就是我习惯的。

sentences.en: Makes you wonder how ESL speakers will sound more and more like LLMs because that's a large part of their exposure.
sentences.zh: 让你想知道 ESL 演讲者如何听起来越来越像法学硕士，因为这是他们曝光的很大一部分。

The funny thing is, In my whole life, this is probably the first time I have used that form, and I wrote it in a completely honest way.

I just want to bang my head to the keyboard.

blocks.html_en:
I can relate, because I also try to understand whether the text I'm reading is written by a human or emitted by a model constantly, and I don't use any LLMs day to day.

On the other hand, I'm not new in the internet, and have relatively thick skin. I'm very amused today by the unintentional attention I got with my cloudy, morning brain.

Nevertheless, regardless of what we do, believe or say, we shall be kind first and remember https://xkcd.com/438/ dearly.

This is what I'm trying to do all the time, but I'm not perfect. Just a human, trying his best.

blocks.html_en:
I'm a customer of Kagi who is in no way affiliated with Kagi, other than sending them money. I agree with bayindirh: It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.

I know that we live in a world where tech increasingly sucks, and positive comments on tech are (correctly!) seen as suspicious. The thing, though, is that Kagi is genuinely just a great product, and search is such a central part of using the Internet that good, trustworthy search makes a huge difference.

blocks.html_en:
>trustworthy search makes a huge difference

That's the rub. Today on the internet, no one can validate that there exists any "trustworthy search". If we don't control the servers and validate the software on it, then we are taking someone's word for it. Most often that someone is a marketing person at the hosting organization. Occasionally it's a random comment on the internet behind a pseudonym we know nothing about.

People are becoming more and more, justifiably, cynical. And it's good for society that they are.

sentences.en: If we don't control the servers and validate the software on it, then we are taking someone's word for it.
sentences.zh: 如果我们不控制服务器并验证服务器上的软件，那么我们就会相信某人的话。

sentences.en: Most often that someone is a marketing person at the hosting organization.
sentences.zh: 最常见的是，某人是托管组织的营销人员。

sentences.en: Occasionally it's a random comment on the internet behind a pseudonym we know nothing about.People are becoming more and more, justifiably, cynical.
sentences.zh: 有时，它是在互联网上以我们一无所知的笔名背后的随机评论。人们变得越来越愤世嫉俗，这是理所当然的。

sentences.en: And it's good for society that they are.
sentences.zh: 它们对社会有好处。

blocks.html_en:
> If we don't control the servers and validate the software on it, then we are taking someone's word for it

That's what trust is, though. I find Kagi worthy of trust since I have no evidence that they are doing anything that would violate my trust in them, and their incentives align with mine. I give them money, they give me good search results.

> People are becoming more and more, justifiably, cynical. And it's good for society that they are.

I do not believe that this is good. Democracies are built on trust. Without trust, they die, as we can plainly see in the US right now, where people are so distrustful of each other that it is starting to destroy the very fabric holding together society itself.

sentences.en: > If we don't control the servers and validate the software on it, then we are taking someone's word for itThat's what trust is, though.
sentences.zh: > 如果我们不控制服务器并验证其上的软件，那么我们就会相信某人的话，但这就是信任。

sentences.en: I find Kagi worthy of trust since I have no evidence that they are doing anything that would violate my trust in them, and their incentives align with mine.
sentences.zh: 我发现卡吉值得信任，因为我没有证据表明他们正在做任何违反我对他们的信任的事情，而且他们的动机与我的一致。

sentences.en: I give them money, they give me good search results.> People are becoming more and more, justifiably, cynical.
sentences.zh: 我给他们钱，他们给我很好的搜索结果。> 人们变得越来越愤世嫉俗，这是理所当然的。

sentences.en: And it's good for society that they are.I do not believe that this is good.
sentences.zh: 他们这样对社会有好处。我不认为这是好事。

sentences.en: Democracies are built on trust.
sentences.zh: 民主是建立在信任之上的。

sentences.en: Without trust, they die, as we can plainly see in the US right now, where people are so distrustful of each other that it is starting to destroy the very fabric holding together society itself.

sentences.zh: 没有信任，他们就会死亡，正如我们现在在美国可以清楚地看到的那样，人们彼此之间如此不信任，以至于开始破坏维系社会本身的结构。

blocks.html_en:
Kagi's business model also aligns incentives better than an ad and/or data mining fueled business model. Of course, this isn't proof, but it is evidence. One can also try the search in areas that one knows deeply and compare it to other search engines to see if it's truly better (I have not done this, but it is possible and isn't just trusting marketing.).

I don't use Kagi primarily because I'm quite averse to subscriptions if I can find another way.

It's a funny claim about me, who doesn't use any LLMs for writing, coding or reducing any kind of mental or manual load.

My comments are on the open, and I can answer any question or comment you have about any of them. :)

No offense taken though. Made me smile while drinking my tea. :)

blocks.html_en:
LLM's are trained on human phrasings. So now we can't use human phrasings anymore?

I am one of those people that used to write em dashes – and now I self-censor them out of my writings :(

sentences.zh: 法学硕士接受过人类措辞方面的培训。

blocks.html_en:
Oh, this appeared late in my feed.

I personally like the ability to filter my own results by promoting, demoting or blocking certain domains to filter lower quality results further. Same for image search which can also filter out AI generated images.

Also, I find lenses very useful (which is a way to limit your search radius) to search for specific things in the specific parts of the internet. i.e. you can define a lens with specific websites for a subject, and if you need something precisely, you can only search these websites.

I recently started to use SlopStop reporting to weed out AI generated webpages from search results, too.

Lastly, all AI features are opt-in. No summaries, no widgets, no extra "bells and whistles" if you don't want on your result page.

sentences.en: Oh, this appeared late in my feed.I personally like the ability to filter my own results by promoting, demoting or blocking certain domains to filter lower quality results further.
sentences.zh: 哦，这在我的提要中出现得很晚。我个人喜欢通过提升、降级或阻止某些域来过滤我自己的结果的能力，以进一步过滤较低质量的结果。

sentences.en: Same for image search which can also filter out AI generated images.Also, I find lenses very useful (which is a way to limit your search radius) to search for specific things in the specific parts of the internet.
sentences.zh: 图像搜索也是如此，它也可以过滤掉人工智能生成的图像。此外，我发现镜头非常有用（这是限制搜索半径的一种方法），可以在互联网的特定部分搜索特定内容。

sentences.en: i.e. you can define a lens with specific websites for a subject, and if you need something precisely, you can only search these websites.I recently started to use SlopStop reporting to weed out AI generated webpages from search results, too.Lastly, all AI features are opt-in.
sentences.zh: 也就是说，你可以为某个主题定义一个带有特定网站的镜头，如果你需要精确的东西，你只能搜索这些网站。我最近开始使用 SlopStop 报告从搜索结果中剔除人工智能生成的网页。最后，所有人工智能功能都是选择性加入的。

sentences.en: No summaries, no widgets, no extra "bells and whistles" if you don't want on your result page.
sentences.zh: 没有摘要，没有小部件，如果您不想在结果页面上显示任何额外的“花哨的东西”。

blocks.html_en:
Oh, this appeared late in my feed.

I personally like the ability to filter my own results by promoting, demoting or blocking certain domains to filter lower quality results further. Same for image search which can also filter out AI generated images.

Also, I find lenses very useful (which is a way to limit your search radius) to search for specific things in the specific parts of the internet. i.e. you can define a lens with specific websites for a subject, and if you need something precisely, you can only search these websites.

I recently started to use SlopStop reporting to weed out AI generated webpages from search results, too.

Lastly, all AI features are opt-in. No summaries, no widgets, no extra "bells and whistles" if you don't want on your result page.

sentences.en: Oh, this appeared late in my feed.I personally like the ability to filter my own results by promoting, demoting or blocking certain domains to filter lower quality results further.
sentences.zh: 哦，这在我的提要中出现得很晚。我个人喜欢通过提升、降级或阻止某些域来过滤我自己的结果的能力，以进一步过滤较低质量的结果。

sentences.en: Same for image search which can also filter out AI generated images.Also, I find lenses very useful (which is a way to limit your search radius) to search for specific things in the specific parts of the internet.
sentences.zh: 图像搜索也是如此，它也可以过滤掉人工智能生成的图像。此外，我发现镜头非常有用（这是限制搜索半径的一种方法），可以在互联网的特定部分搜索特定内容。

sentences.en: i.e. you can define a lens with specific websites for a subject, and if you need something precisely, you can only search these websites.I recently started to use SlopStop reporting to weed out AI generated webpages from search results, too.Lastly, all AI features are opt-in.
sentences.zh: 也就是说，你可以为某个主题定义一个带有特定网站的镜头，如果你需要精确的东西，你只能搜索这些网站。我最近开始使用 SlopStop 报告从搜索结果中剔除人工智能生成的网页。最后，所有人工智能功能都是选择性加入的。

sentences.en: No summaries, no widgets, no extra "bells and whistles" if you don't want on your result page.
sentences.zh: 没有摘要，没有小部件，如果您不想在结果页面上显示任何额外的“花哨的东西”。

sentences.en: Oh, this appeared late in my feed.

blocks.html_en: I personally like the ability to filter my own results by promoting, demoting or blocking certain domains to filter lower quality results further. Same for image search which can also filter out AI generated images.

sentences.en: I personally like the ability to filter my own results by promoting, demoting or blocking certain domains to filter lower quality results further.
sentences.zh: 我个人喜欢通过提升、降级或阻止某些域来过滤我自己的结果，以进一步过滤较低质量的结果。

sentences.en: Same for image search which can also filter out AI generated images.
sentences.zh: 图像搜索也是如此，它也可以过滤掉人工智能生成的图像。

blocks.html_en: Also, I find lenses very useful (which is a way to limit your search radius) to search for specific things in the specific parts of the internet. i.e. you can define a lens with specific websites for a subject, and if you need something precisely, you can only search these websites.

sentences.en: Also, I find lenses very useful (which is a way to limit your search radius) to search for specific things in the specific parts of the internet.
sentences.zh: 另外，我发现镜头对于在互联网的特定部分搜索特定内容非常有用（这是限制搜索半径的一种方法）。

sentences.en: i.e. you can define a lens with specific websites for a subject, and if you need something precisely, you can only search these websites.
sentences.zh: 也就是说，你可以为某个主题定义一个特定网站的镜头，如果你需要精确的东西，你只能搜索这些网站。

blocks.html_en: I recently started to use SlopStop reporting to weed out AI generated webpages from search results, too.

sentences.en: I recently started to use SlopStop reporting to weed out AI generated webpages from search results, too.
sentences.zh: 我最近也开始使用 SlopStop 报告从搜索结果中清除人工智能生成的网页。

blocks.html_en: Lastly, all AI features are opt-in. No summaries, no widgets, no extra "bells and whistles" if you don't want on your result page.

sentences.en: Lastly, all AI features are opt-in.
sentences.zh: 最后，所有人工智能功能都是可选的。

sentences.en: No summaries, no widgets, no extra "bells and whistles" if you don't want on your result page.
sentences.zh: 没有摘要，没有小部件，如果您不想在结果页面上显示任何额外的“花哨的东西”。

eww

It's one thing to throw in a, "hey, I've used this service and I recommend it to others," but this is just gross, blatant advertising.

> It’s possibly the single most impactful thing in my digital life.

Even if this is true, only an LLM would ever believe posting this sentence would come off as anything other than bullshit lol

blocks.html_en:
Thanks for your opinion about my opinion of a service and its effects on my life.

If you can provide me your contact details, I'd love to send my similar comments to you and get them cleared by you to satisfy your standards of endorsement.

For your information and entertainment, ZeroGPT returns 97% human for that whole comment which I wrote while having my breakfast and my phone was laying on the table.

Unbelievable.

sentences.zh: 感谢您对我对服务及其对我生活的影响的看法。如果您能向我提供您的联系方式，我很乐意将我的类似评论发送给您，并让您清除这些评论，以满足您的认可标准。为了您的信息和娱乐，ZeroGPT 对我在吃早餐时写的整个评论（我的手机放在桌子上）的回复率为 97%。难以置信。

blocks.html_en:
Is Kagi still using Russian government owned tech (Yandex) that censors informations about the illegal Ukraine war crimes ?

If so, even if I do agree with your claims about the need to avoid ads so that the user is not the product, I believe there's a double standard in action here.

I really liked the product before having to cancel my subscription for this reason.

Now I'm stuck with boring Qwant and Ecosia...

Well hopefully Oursie.com based on Staan with a subscription system will be a good alternative soon.

sentences.en: Is Kagi still using Russian government owned tech (Yandex) that censors informations about the illegal Ukraine war crimes ?If so, even if I do agree with your claims about the need to avoid ads so that the user is not the product, I believe there's a double standard in action here.I really liked the product before having to cancel my subscription for this reason.Now I'm stuck with boring Qwant and Ecosia...Well hopefully Oursie.com based on Staan with a subscription system will be a good alternative soon.
sentences.zh: Kagi 是否仍在使用俄罗斯政府拥有的技术（Yandex）来审查有关乌克兰非法战争罪行的信息？如果是这样，即使我确实同意您关于需要避免广告以便用户不是产品的说法，我相信这里存在双重标准。在因此不得不取消订阅之前我真的很喜欢该产品。现在我希望继续使用无聊的 Qwant 和 Ecosia...基于 Staan 的带有订阅系统的 Oursie.com 将很快成为一个不错的选择。

blocks.html_en:
As a Ukrainian user of Kagi, their partnership with Yandex is unfortunate, since they are indirectly funding the war, but at the same time I can totally see why it makes sense for the service they're providing

With a service like that you want as much diversification as possible, since western search engines can get heavily censored too. Even if they aren't as censored right now, this might change at any moment with us not even noticing it

For example, right now Yandex tends to be much more lenient to piracy, since it's basically legal in Russia

sentences.en: As a Ukrainian user of Kagi, their partnership with Yandex is unfortunate, since they are indirectly funding the war, but at the same time I can totally see why it makes sense for the service they're providingWith a service like that you want as much diversification as possible, since western search engines can get heavily censored too.
sentences.zh: 作为 Kagi 的乌克兰用户，他们与 Yandex 的合作是不幸的，因为他们间接资助了战争，但同时我完全明白为什么他们提供的服务有意义，因为西方搜索引擎也可能受到严格审查，因此您希望提供尽可能多样化的服务。

sentences.en: Even if they aren't as censored right now, this might change at any moment with us not even noticing itFor example, right now Yandex tends to be much more lenient to piracy, since it's basically legal in Russia
sentences.zh: 即使他们现在没有受到如此审查，这种情况也可能随时发生变化，我们甚至都没有注意到它例如，现在 Yandex 对盗版往往更加宽容，因为盗版在俄罗斯基本上是合法的

blocks.html_en:
> For example, right now Yandex tends to be much more lenient to piracy, since it's basically legal in Russia

Likely because most of the pirated content is western and therefor both inconsequential and a way to thumb their noses at the "west."

blocks.html_en:
Yandex is only for image search. Implementing a "I don't want to fund Yandex" option would solve the problem for customers trying not to fund this war.

This would even lower the cost of the user's actions.

Not doing so is a strong take.

blocks.html_en:
Well there are millions of European trying to avoid US tech. It's hard. A lot harder than Russian tech.

As for the comparison, I don't think it's fair. The US has not yet invaded its neighbor, Canada, leading to millions of deaths and heavy casualties. Let's not adopt your binary view on this matter.

sentences.en: It's hard.
sentences.zh: 这很难。

sentences.en: A lot harder than Russian tech.As for the comparison, I don't think it's fair.
sentences.zh: 比俄罗斯技术难很多。至于比较，我认为不公平。

sentences.en: The US has not yet invaded its neighbor, Canada, leading to millions of deaths and heavy casualties.
sentences.zh: 美国尚未入侵其邻国加拿大，导致数百万人死亡和重大人员伤亡。

sentences.en: Let's not adopt your binary view on this matter.
sentences.zh: 我们在这个问题上不要采取二元观点。

blocks.html_en:
So it's ok if they invade far-away countries, napalm bomb them leading to millions of casualties, but not ok to invade neighboring countries?

The US is responsible for far more pain and suffering in my nock of the world, so it's funny when Americans act like they have some sort of moral high ground.

sentences.en: So it's ok if they invade far-away countries, napalm bomb them leading to millions of casualties, but not ok to invade neighboring countries?The US is responsible for far more pain and suffering in my nock of the world, so it's funny when Americans act like they have some sort of moral high ground.
sentences.zh: 那么，如果他们入侵遥远的国家，用凝固汽油弹轰炸它们，导致数百万人伤亡，那是可以的，但入侵邻国就不行了？美国对我这个世界上更多的痛苦负有责任，所以当美国人表现得好像他们拥有某种道德制高点时，这很有趣。

blocks.html_en:
Given that we talk about mostly white European settlers, just the huge majority of native american lands, a good chunk of Mexico, Hawaii, Puerto Rico, and several smaller places, areas controlled as "banana republics", Phillipines, and who knows, eventually Greenland, and even talk about Canada, unserious as it likely is.

And with no own ethnic population there to begin with or continuous historical ties to the area.

sentences.en: Given that we talk about mostly white European settlers, just the huge majority of native american lands, a good chunk of Mexico, Hawaii, Puerto Rico, and several smaller places, areas controlled as "banana republics", Phillipines, and who knows, eventually Greenland, and even talk about Canada, unserious as it likely is.And with no own ethnic population there to begin with or continuous historical ties to the area.
sentences.zh: 鉴于我们谈论的大多是欧洲白人定居者，只是绝大多数美洲原住民土地，墨西哥的大部分地区，夏威夷，波多黎各，以及几个较小的地方，被控制为“香蕉共和国”的地区，菲律宾，谁知道，最终是格陵兰岛，甚至谈论加拿大，尽管它可能并不严肃。而且那里一开始就没有自己的种族人口，也没有与该地区持续的历史联系。

blocks.html_en:
The US invaded Afghanistan, Iraq, Vietnam, Syria, Latin America etc.

The list is too long. So here's a link showing the list of invasions, bombings, and CIA-backed regime-change operations in other countries:

https://www.davemanuel.com/us-military-interventions-invasio...

The estimates are that the US has killed between 20 and 30 million people after WWII.

And these are just direct kills. The figure is much higher if you count indirect kills through means of economic sanctions.

blocks.html_en:
> You're comparing a war currently being played by Russia, destroying their own country by the way, to past wars.

During the past year and a half, the US has bombed Iran, Venezuela, Iraq, Yemen and Mexico. It has provided free bombs to a country that has been bombing Palestine (genocide), Lebanon, Syria, Yemen and Iran.

It has kidnapped the leader of a country and assassinated the leader of another country.

It has pirated all oil shipments to Cuba, causing a severe crisis https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_United_States_fuel_blocka...

blocks.html_en: The US bombed a school? Where's the worldwide outrage in reaction to Russia destroying HUNDREDS of them in Ukraine? While everyone is hyperfixated on the US, Russia has deliberately targeted schools, hospitals, kindergartens, supermarkets, apartment buildings and far more in targeted terror campaigns. And yet all is silent in the West, in fact every attempt to bring up the evil of Russia is immediately drowned out by BUT WHAT ABOUT. Every single time. It's because this isn't really about schools. This just lets people quietly signal their support for Russia for them standing up to 'the West' by murdering hundreds of thousands of people. Whatever evil the US has committed in the past and is supporting now, Russia has done tenfold in just this war. The list of kidnappings, plundering, torture and rape that they've committed is practically endless. They have no mercy, but many Westerners have had their brains melted by east-vs-west 'discourse' and they won't ever understand.

sentences.en: The US bombed a school?

I have no idea why you're phrasing this like this when so much money is being put into solving precisely this problem right now

blocks.html_en:
Why are you trying to divert the topic?

You don't think the genocide in Ukraine has enough scale of a tragedy, with more than 2 million casualties since 2022? Isn't this enough to flag a product?

The Ukrainians posed no threat to anyone, they didn't have a nuclear program, in fact they surrendered the third largest nuclear stockpile and one of the signatures of the memorandum is trying to delete Ukraine.

Why are you trying to dilute this event which is directly related to the topic?

Is it to try to normalize the crime of genocide in Ukraine?

sentences.en: Why are you trying to divert the topic?You don't think the genocide in Ukraine has enough scale of a tragedy, with more than 2 million casualties since 2022?
sentences.zh: 你为什么要转移话题？你认为乌克兰的种族灭绝悲剧规模还不够大吗？自2022年以来，伤亡人数已超过200万？

sentences.en: Isn't this enough to flag a product?The Ukrainians posed no threat to anyone, they didn't have a nuclear program, in fact they surrendered the third largest nuclear stockpile and one of the signatures of the memorandum is trying to delete Ukraine.Why are you trying to dilute this event which is directly related to the topic?Is it to try to normalize the crime of genocide in Ukraine?
sentences.zh: 这还不足以标记一个产品吗？乌克兰人对任何人都没有构成威胁，他们没有核计划，事实上他们交出了第三大核储备，备忘录的签名之一就是试图删除乌克兰。你为什么要试图淡化这个与主题直接相关的事件？是试图使乌克兰的种族灭绝罪正常化吗？

blocks.html_en:
Because your argument suffers the fallacy of proving too much. You're trying to say that all Russian technology must be boycotted because it does a war, but your argument also says American technology must be boycotted, Chinese technology must be boycotted and European technology must be boycotted. No transistors for anyone who subscribes to your argument!

https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/G5eMM3Wp3hbCuKKPE/proving-to...

sentences.en: Because your argument suffers the fallacy of proving too much.
sentences.zh: 因为你的论点存在证明太多的谬误。

sentences.en: You're trying to say that all Russian technology must be boycotted because it does a war, but your argument also says American technology must be boycotted, Chinese technology must be boycotted and European technology must be boycotted.
sentences.zh: 你想说的是，所有俄罗斯技术都必须受到抵制，因为它会引发战争，但你的论点还说，美国技术必须受到抵制，中国技术必须受到抵制，欧洲技术也必须受到抵制。

sentences.en: No transistors for anyone who subscribes to your argument!https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/G5eMM3Wp3hbCuKKPE/proving-to...
sentences.zh: 对于任何订阅你的论点的人来说，没有晶体管！https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/G5eMM3Wp3hbCuKKPE/proving-to...

blocks.html_en:
No, it's simpler than that - there's an ongoing genocide in Ukraine, a country that was at peace and posed threat to no one.

The guys that surrendered their nukes in exchange for security, right?

What I'm saying is that anything that helps prolong this war should be avoided.

It's not a fallacy, it's actually common sense.

You're again trying to dilute the genocide by bringing USA, China, etc.

So why are you trying to normalize one event, by diluting it among other events that have no relationship whatsoever with it?

blocks.html_en:
It matters within the context of this comment thread about a company using assets from specific state commiting genocide that had caused more than 2 million casualties in 4 years.

We're talking about cities razed to the ground, more than a thousand medical facilities destroyed, and of course the systematic and deliberate attempt destruction of a culture - just because they wanted to be a sovereign democracy.

The difference is information manipulation, like misinformation.

While there should be a complete sanction of Russian products, cod is cod, it's a consumable that has to follow public health standards.

There's no such thing for information, and for many years the Russian state has been trying hard at it.

blocks.html_en:
Well the last time the US committed genocide of a culture was way before any tech company was founded, while Russia is doing it since 2022 - by their own admission.

If we count of course the illegal annexation of territory, one of the most egregious crimes against sovereignty, it's been doing it since the occupation of Moldova, Georgia, Ukraine in 2014, you get the idea.

What I don't understand is why you're trying to divert the attention to the US, the topic regarding the current crimes against humanity and war crimes of Russia have enough merits to be a topic on its own.

sentences.en: Well the last time the US committed genocide of a culture was way before any tech company was founded, while Russia is doing it since 2022 - by their own admission.If we count of course the illegal annexation of territory, one of the most egregious crimes against sovereignty, it's been doing it since the occupation of Moldova, Georgia, Ukraine in 2014, you get the idea.What I don't understand is why you're trying to divert the attention to the US, the topic regarding the current crimes against humanity and war crimes of Russia have enough merits to be a topic on its own.
sentences.zh: 好吧，上一次美国对一种文化进行种族灭绝是在任何科技公司成立之前，而俄罗斯自 2022 年就开始这样做了——他们自己也承认。如果我们当然算上非法吞并领土，这是最严重的侵犯主权罪行之一，自 2014 年占领摩尔多瓦、格鲁吉亚、乌克兰以来，它就一直在这样做，你就明白了。我不明白的是，你为什么试图将注意力转移到美国，这个话题与当前的话题有关。俄罗斯的反人类罪和战争罪本身就有足够的优点来成为一个话题。

blocks.html_en:
Well Russia Today isn't really a news source. It's more of a propaganda outlet. But so are most of the sources that we call news, anyway. Maybe if high quality news sources existed, Kagi could uprank them, but they don't.

This isn't similar to the Yandex situation because Yandex actually returns good results and Russia Today is garbage.

blocks.html_en:
1. Yandex is dutch controlled, the russia-related business got sold in 2024 to Russian investors.

2. Yandex was never owned by the Russian government, albeit government-related censorship happened (but so does on Twitter/Google/Meta etc as they all have to comply with local laws where they operate).

3. Kagi answers questions about Russian war crimes. You can try yourself [1]

4. There's no such thing as a legal war, the closest is a UN sanctioned peacekeeping operation.

[1] https://kagi.com/search?q=what+war+crimes+did+russia+commit+...

Is it? I can't find an official source that supports that claim. Also, Yandex disinvested from Russia and split the non-Russian business into a different company. So is it Yandex or "Yandex (copy)"?

I'm not trying to defend / take a position. I just find it difficult to reason without having an official (up-to-date) source.

sentences.en: Is it?

sentences.en: I can't find an official source that supports that claim.
sentences.zh: 我找不到支持该说法的官方消息来源。

sentences.en: Also, Yandex disinvested from Russia and split the non-Russian business into a different company.
sentences.zh: 此外，Yandex 撤出了俄罗斯的投资，并将非俄罗斯业务拆分为另一家公司。

Trying to say that Yandex isn't a Russian company is preposterous. It's the largest search engine in Russia and runs tons of other online services that are analogous to social media and cloud things in the English and Western European language speaking world.

blocks.html_en:
no it didn't. the international entity sold off the vast majority of yandex businesses and assets to russian investors, then rebranded to "nebius group". that is to say that the yandex index/api used by kagi is wholly russian-owned and has essentially doubled down on russia/its allies.

if you're curious, the dominant business retained by the international entity was their data center stuff (though yandex still operates its own cloud business in russia/elsewhere).

sentences.en: no it didn't.
sentences.zh: 不，没有。

sentences.zh: 该国际实体将 yandex 的绝大多数业务和资产出售给俄罗斯投资者，然后更名为“nebius group”。

blocks.html_en:
I'm not saying I agree with it, but I think this comment

> Yandex does not add any special feature or functionality that is so great they should make it obligatory to include as a default option.

kind of captures kagi's argument: that its removal would degrade search results in a not-immediately-noticeable way, particularly for certain areas of the world, which might actually be worse than a loss of a distinct add-on feature or something.

...but yeah, from my perspective, it'd be great if you could turn it off.

blocks.html_en: Unfortunately it appears to not be a joke, my best theory is that either the persons running it have sympathies with Russia and their war efforts, or they value the marginal gain in revenue from having Yandex integrated in some sort of ultra cut throat free market capitalism approach with no qualms or concerns about anything ethical or moral.

sentences.en: Unfortunately it appears to not be a joke, my best theory is that either the persons running it have sympathies with Russia and their war efforts, or they value the marginal gain in revenue from having Yandex integrated in some sort of ultra cut throat free market capitalism approach with no qualms or concerns about anything ethical or moral.

blocks.html_en:
Consider that perhaps the person you're responding to really does treat Russian and the US equally... by continuing to import goods from both, and use technology or services from both countries.

Many people in Europe eat Russian fish, warm their houses with Russian natural gas, use American SaaS, and fill their houses with goods made in China.

That doesn't mean they support everything those countries do.

It does mean their solution for nation-state level conflicts isn't a total embargo of the country.

sentences.en: Consider that perhaps the person you're responding to really does treat Russian and the US equally...
sentences.zh: 考虑一下，也许您所回复的人确实平等对待俄罗斯和美国......

sentences.en: by continuing to import goods from both, and use technology or services from both countries.Many people in Europe eat Russian fish, warm their houses with Russian natural gas, use American SaaS, and fill their houses with goods made in China.That doesn't mean they support everything those countries do.It does mean their solution for nation-state level conflicts isn't a total embargo of the country.
sentences.zh: 继续从两国进口商品，并使用两国的技术或服务。许多欧洲人吃俄罗斯的鱼，用俄罗斯的天然气取暖，使用美国的SaaS，并用中国制造的商品装满他们的房子。这并不意味着他们支持这些国家所做的一切。这确实意味着他们解决民族国家层面冲突的方案并不是对该国的全面禁运。

blocks.html_en:
The two comments above mine is a clear example on how mob mentality works. Albeit an impotent mob rage among anonymous internet commentators, but the exact same mentality that IRL usually escalates into violence and later into the killing of a designated victim(s).

Lies and accusations get enforced and exaggerated at every step. From purchasing services from a Russian IT company -> " involved in russian war affairs" -> "the persons running it have sympathies with Russia and their war efforts".

I really thought Europeans got this kind of mentality out of their system in the 20th century, but it's alive and well still. And there will be innocent people killed as a consequence. And people here on HN will celebrate and rejoice in that. They know they are on "the right side", and therefore have the right to do anything to anyone. Just like Mr Putin might think over on his side.

sentences.en: The two comments above mine is a clear example on how mob mentality works.
sentences.zh: 我上面的两条评论是暴民心态如何运作的一个明显例子。

sentences.en: Albeit an impotent mob rage among anonymous internet commentators, but the exact same mentality that IRL usually escalates into violence and later into the killing of a designated victim(s).Lies and accusations get enforced and exaggerated at every step.
sentences.zh: 尽管匿名网络评论员的愤怒无能为力，但这与现实生活中通常升级为暴力并随后杀害指定受害者的心态完全相同。谎言和指控每一步都被强制和夸大。

sentences.en: From purchasing services from a Russian IT company -> " involved in russian war affairs" -> "the persons running it have sympathies with Russia and their war efforts".I really thought Europeans got this kind of mentality out of their system in the 20th century, but it's alive and well still.
sentences.zh: 从购买俄罗斯IT公司的服务->“参与俄罗斯战争事务”->“该公司的经营者对俄罗斯及其战争努力表示同情”。我真的认为欧洲人在20世纪已经将这种心态从他们的系统中剔除，但它仍然存在。

sentences.en: And there will be innocent people killed as a consequence.
sentences.zh: 结果将会有无辜者被杀。

sentences.en: And people here on HN will celebrate and rejoice in that.
sentences.zh: HN 的人们会为此庆祝和高兴。

sentences.en: They know they are on "the right side", and therefore have the right to do anything to anyone.
sentences.zh: 他们知道自己站在“正确的一边”，因此有权对任何人做任何事。

sentences.en: Just like Mr Putin might think over on his side.
sentences.zh: 就像普京先生可能会站在他这边思考一样。

What makes you think Kagi is owned by the Russian state? Should we cancel all US services because the US government started the Iran war for no reason?

blocks.html_en: Keep fighting the good fight. Also, offering a counterpoint for the sibling comments: Yandex results are the most valuable for the type of thing censored in all other search engines. If I want to search for news on the war on Ukraine I go anywhere, but the things yandex works for are censored everywhere else.

sentences.en: Keep fighting the good fight.
sentences.zh: 继续打好仗。

sentences.en: Also, offering a counterpoint for the sibling comments: Yandex results are the most valuable for the type of thing censored in all other search engines.
sentences.zh: 另外，为兄弟评论提供一个对立点：Yandex 结果对于所有其他搜索引擎中审查的内容类型来说是最有价值的。

sentences.en: If I want to search for news on the war on Ukraine I go anywhere, but the things yandex works for are censored everywhere else.
sentences.zh: 如果我想搜索有关乌克兰战争的新闻，我可以去任何地方，但 yandex 提供的内容在其他地方都会受到审查。

blocks.html_en: Also, people seem to be oblivious to the existence of the neo-cloud: Nebius whose services and servers, a lot of western AI companies use. Nebius was spun out of Yandex 2 years ago to assuage the "supporting Russia" allegations and is still run and managed by an ex-Yandex crew. By that measure, I suppose using a lot of western AI tools is also equivalent to "supporting Russia".

sentences.en: Also, people seem to be oblivious to the existence of the neo-cloud: Nebius whose services and servers, a lot of western AI companies use.
sentences.zh: 此外，人们似乎忽视了新云的存在：很多西方人工智能公司都使用 Nebius 的服务和服务器。

blocks.html_en: Good thing we have Kagi in the middle to firewall it from us, then. It does genuinely return better results for westerners due to not being subject to western censorship. I assume Kagi is smart enough to, say, down-rank or ignore Yandex results when the search is about Ukraine

sentences.en: Good thing we have Kagi in the middle to firewall it from us, then.
sentences.zh: 幸好我们有卡吉在中间，将它挡在我们面前。

sentences.en: It does genuinely return better results for westerners due to not being subject to western censorship.
sentences.zh: 由于不受西方审查制度的影响，它确实为西方人带来了更好的结果。

sentences.en: I assume Kagi is smart enough to, say, down-rank or ignore Yandex results when the search is about Ukraine
sentences.zh: 我认为 Kagi 足够聪明，可以在搜索有关乌克兰时降低排名或忽略 Yandex 结果

blocks.html_en: It is not really inconsistent. Despite all the flaws of the current US government, USA is still a relatively free democracy. Russia is a full-blown fascist dictatorship. Russia is also a much more acute threat to me than Trump's USA, because I am from central European country formerly in Soviet Union's sphere of influence, that would be next on the chopping block if Ukraine falls.

sentences.en: It is not really inconsistent.
sentences.zh: 这其实并不矛盾。

sentences.en: Russia is a full-blown fascist dictatorship.
sentences.zh: 俄罗斯是一个彻底的法西斯独裁国家。

sentences.en: Russia is also a much more acute threat to me than Trump's USA, because I am from central European country formerly in Soviet Union's sphere of influence, that would be next on the chopping block if Ukraine falls.

blocks.html_en:
FWIW I don't think anything Kagi is doing is unethical. If I did - if I did feel it was unethical to spend money at a company that is in a country that does war crimes - I'd also have to feel that almost all spending was unethical, including things like buying lunch.

On a secondary point, if we want more ethical things to exist, we have to create them. If we want an ethical alternative to Mullvad it's on us to do it.

sentences.en: FWIW I don't think anything Kagi is doing is unethical.
sentences.zh: FWIW我不认为卡吉所做的任何事情都是不道德的。

sentences.en: If I did - if I did feel it was unethical to spend money at a company that is in a country that does war crimes - I'd also have to feel that almost all spending was unethical, including things like buying lunch.On a secondary point, if we want more ethical things to exist, we have to create them.
sentences.zh: 如果我这样做了——如果我确实觉得在一个犯有战争罪的国家的公司花钱是不道德的——我也会觉得几乎所有的支出都是不道德的，包括买午餐之类的事情。其次，如果我们想要更多道德的东西存在，我们就必须创造它们。

sentences.en: If we want an ethical alternative to Mullvad it's on us to do it.
sentences.zh: 如果我们想要一个道德的替代方案来替代 Mulvad，我们就必须这样做。

blocks.html_en: I think the situation you're in affects what you can reasonably do and hence what can be considered "ethical". You cannot opt out of paying taxes (and buying lunch), but it is possible here for me to avoid funding this war and try to seek better alternatives.

sentences.zh: 我认为你所处的情况会影响你可以合理地做什么，从而影响什么可以被视为“道德”。

sentences.en: You cannot opt out of paying taxes (and buying lunch), but it is possible here for me to avoid funding this war and try to seek better alternatives.

Chinese phone-makers and Yandex are behaving pretty differently. China and Russia are also behaving pretty differently.

There's no clear reason why someone couldn't have qualms about one but not the other.

blocks.html_en:
I've been a kagi subscriber for a few months now, and I really want to recommend Kagi to everyone, but the results and index (so far) have been sadly lacking.

I had to use post-nerf, post-lobotomy 2026 Google in quite a few cases.

blocks.html_en:
Free user onboarding is awful. Ios safari.

Half the screen is the kagi sign up ad. Not a good show of the product.

Then it’s all black on white. 2026. Color. Contrast. It’s one of the pillars. Google figured this day 1:Dark blue links. Then the line spacing is too high. Information density doesn’t feel right.

Overall 2/10 experience for a 10/10 product.
